title: ⚔️ Chapter boss ⭐⭐⭐: my day, my family

questions.prompt: Complete: "I ___ got a bike. I always walk to school."
questions.explanation: The negative of "I have got" is "I haven't got": have + not. "Hasn't" is only for he/she/it, and "don't got" or "isn't got" mix other auxiliaries into the "have got" structure.

questions.prompt: Complete: "After dinner, my mother ___ TV with us."
questions.explanation: "My mother" = she, so the verb takes the third-person ending. Verbs ending in -ch add -es, not just -s: she watches TV ✓. The common trap is writing "watchs" — after -ch, -sh or -o, the ending is always -es (watches, washes, goes).

questions.prompt: Complete the question: "___ do you go to bed?" — "At nine o'clock."
questions.explanation: The answer "At nine o'clock" gives a clock time, so the question word is "What time": What time do you go to bed? "How many time" is not correct English, "How old" asks about age, and "Where" asks about a place.

questions.prompt: "School starts at half past eight." What time is that on the clock?
questions.explanation: "Half past eight" means 30 minutes after eight o'clock, so it is 8:30 ✓. The common trap is confusing "half" (30 minutes) with "a quarter" (15 minutes): 8:15 is a quarter past eight and 8:45 is a quarter to nine. 9:30 would be half past nine.

questions.prompt: Which sentence puts the adverb in the correct place?

options.text: Always Rania gets up at six.
Rania gets always up at six.
Rania gets up always at six.
Rania always gets up at six.

questions.explanation: An adverb of frequency goes BEFORE the main verb: Rania always gets up at six ✓. The common trap is putting it after the verb ("gets always up") or at the start of the sentence — with an ordinary verb like "get up", "always" must sit just before it.

questions.prompt: Complete the short answer: "Has Youssef got a computer?" — "No, he ___."
questions.explanation: The short answer repeats the auxiliary of the question in the negative: Has he…? → No, he hasn't ✓. The common trap is answering "No, he doesn't" — that matches a "Does he have…?" question, not a "Has he got…?" question. "Haven't" is for I/you/we/they.
